label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out how many lockers are open after 100 people each toggle the lockers in a certain way. Let me start by understanding the problem step by step. First, there are 100 lockers, all initially closed. Then there are 100 people. Each person k, from 1 to 100, toggles every k-th locker. Toggling means if the locker is closed, they open it, and if it's open, they close it. The first person is Person 1, who opens every locker. Then Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker, which would close lockers 2, 4, 6, etc. Then Person 3 toggles every 3rd locker, and so on up to Person 100. The question is: How many lockers are open at the end? Hmm. Let me think about how each locker is toggled. For a specific locker number, say locker number n, how many times is it toggled? Well, each time a person k comes by where k divides n. Because if k divides n, then the k-th person will toggle the n-th locker. For example, locker 6 is toggled by person 1, 2, 3, and 6. So the number of times a locker is toggled is equal to the number of divisors of that locker's number. Since all lockers start closed, a locker will end up open if it is toggled an odd number of times. Because each toggle switches the state: closed to open, open to closed. So if it's toggled even times, it ends up closed again; odd times, open. Therefore, the problem reduces to finding how many numbers from 1 to 100 have an odd number of divisors. Because those numbers will be toggled an odd number of times, ending up open. But wait, most numbers have divisors in pairs. For example, 6 has divisors 1 and 6, 2 and 3. So that's four divisors, which is even. The only time a number has an odd number of divisors is when one of the divisors is repeated, i.e., when the number is a perfect square.
